title: Cybersecurity & Ethical Hacking Foundations
subtitle: Threats, defenses, and the offensive thinking that makes both sides better
description: A foundations course in modern cybersecurity for engineers and curious learners. Covers attacker thinking, the OWASP Top 10, network security, authentication, cryptography basics, and the legal and ethical framework for security work. Practical patterns, real-world incidents, and the threat model every engineer should carry around.
audience: Engineers, IT pros, and curious learners. Comfortable with the command line; understands HTTP basics; wants to build security intuition rather than memorize tool flags.
prerequisites: Have a willingness to be patient with how much there is to learn
outcomes: Think like an attacker — and defend accordingly
Recognize and prevent OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities
Understand authentication, sessions, and what breaks them
Apply cryptography correctly (and avoid mis-using it)
Know the basics of incident response
Operate inside the legal and ethical bounds of security work

modules.title: Threat models and attacker thinking
modules.objective: How to think like an attacker before you defend.

lessons.title: What is a threat model?
Reconnaissance: what the internet already knows
The CIA triad: confidentiality, integrity, availability
Legal and ethical lines you cannot cross

modules.title: Web application security
modules.objective: The vulnerabilities that take down most apps.

lessons.title: Injection: SQL, command, template
XSS, CSRF, and the browser security model
Auth & sessions: how they break
OWASP Top 10 — what to memorize and what to look up

modules.title: Network and system security
modules.objective: Securing the layers below the app.

lessons.title: Network reconnaissance: nmap, netcat, basics
Firewalls, VPNs, and segmentation
OS hardening: users, services, attack surface
Supply chain risks: dependencies and CI/CD

modules.title: Cryptography in practice
modules.objective: Use crypto correctly without becoming a cryptographer.

lessons.title: Symmetric vs asymmetric and when to use which
Hashing, salts, and password storage
TLS: how HTTPS actually works
Pitfalls: never roll your own crypto

modules.title: Operations: detection and response
modules.objective: Knowing when you've been hit and what to do.

lessons.title: Logging and detection: what to capture
Incident response: the SANS phases
Bug bounty and responsible disclosure
Building a security mindset that lasts
